text: My name is Norman Duncan. I was nineteen when I jumped into Normandy. I was with the 101st Airborne. We came in just before dawn on the sixth of June. It rained all night in the plane. I remember the green light, and then I remember the wind. Most of the boys I trained with didn't make it past the first week. My mother wrote me every day. I still have some of the letters. I don't talk about the war much. I never have. But I think about Sergeant Williams every single day. He was from Ohio. He had three kids.
title: Norman Duncan · D-Day · 101st Airborne
speaker: Norman Duncan

fragment.text: It rained all night in the plane.
The green light, then the wind.
Most of the boys I trained with didn't make it past the first week.
Sergeant Williams, from Ohio, three kids
My mother wrote me every day. I still have some of the letters.
I don't talk about the war much. I never have.

fact.claim: The 101st Airborne Division jumped into Normandy before dawn on June 6, 1944.

sources.title: U.S. Army Center of Military History — 101st Airborne in Normandy
National WWII Museum — D-Day Airborne Operations

fact.imageCredit: Robert F. Sargent, U.S. Coast Guard — 'Into the Jaws of Death', Omaha Beach, June 6 1944 (public domain, NARA)

fact.claim: Paratroopers were given a green light signal to jump.

text: He uses plain nouns — 'the green light, then the wind.' I should not embellish that. Keep his rhythm: short sentences, no adjectives.
The emotional center is Sergeant Williams, not himself. He deflects to other people. Honor that.
Dear child I will never meet —
I was nineteen when I jumped into France.
It rained all night in the plane…

letter.salutation: Dear child I will never meet,

sections.heading: The morning it rained on us
sections.body: I was nineteen when I jumped into France. It rained all night in the plane. I remember the green light, and then I remember the wind. That is almost all I remember of the first hour. I tell you this not because it is brave — it isn't — but because you should know that most of what we did was wait, and be cold, and then do the thing we had practiced.[^1]
sections.imageCredit: Robert F. Sargent, U.S. Coast Guard — 'Into the Jaws of Death', Omaha Beach, June 6 1944 (public domain, NARA)

footnotes.text: 101st Airborne, Operation Overlord, June 6, 1944 — verified via U.S. Army Center of Military History.

sections.heading: Sergeant Williams
sections.body: There was a man named Williams from Ohio. He had three children. Most of the boys I trained with didn't make it past the first week, and he was one of them. I think about him every single day. I want you to know his name, because he was a real person, and because I don't think anyone else still says it.

sections.heading: What my mother did
sections.body: My mother wrote me every day. Every day. I still have some of the letters. You come from her, in a line that goes mother to son to son to daughter to you. The war was long ago for you. It is still close to me. Both things are true.

letter.signoff:
With love, across the years —
Norman
letter.speaker: Norman Duncan
